note: IB Diploma Programme Group 1 — Studies in Language and Literature. Two courses: Language A: Literature, and Language A: Language and Literature. Both offered at SL and HL. Assessment model from first examinations 2021.

topics.subject: English A: Literature
topics.description: SL: one guided analysis of an unseen text. HL: two guided analyses of two unseen texts (one prose, one poetry). Assessment of how language, structure, and form create meaning.

topics.subject: English A: Literature
topics.description: Comparative essay on two works from the reading list, responding to one of four questions. Assessment of comparative literary analysis, thesis development, and textual evidence.

topics.subject: English A: Literature
topics.description: 15-minute recorded oral: 10-minute commentary on a student-chosen extract from a studied work, followed by 5-minute discussion. Assessment of sustained close reading and articulation of literary ideas.

topics.subject: English A: Literature
topics.description: Independent formal literary essay of 1,200–1,500 words on one of the works studied. Student-chosen focus; assessed for depth of analysis, originality, and literary understanding.

topics.subject: English A: Literature
topics.description: SL: minimum 4 works across at least 3 literary forms, including at least 1 work in translation. HL: minimum 6 works across at least 3 literary forms, including at least 2 works in translation. Works must span different time periods and cultures.

topics.subject: English A: Language and Literature
topics.description: Analysis of non-literary texts (media, advertising, speeches, journalism). Assessment of how language choices, register, tone, and context construct meaning for specific audiences.

topics.subject: English A: Language and Literature
topics.description: Comparative essay responding to a prompt about two literary works. Assessment of ability to compare how different texts construct meaning in their social and cultural contexts.

topics.subject: English A: Language and Literature
topics.description: Oral commentary on a student-chosen extract from one non-literary and one literary text studied; focus on a global issue present in both. Assessment of close reading, argumentation, and oral expression.
